examples.title: Basic Deadlock - Two Threads Two Locks
examples.description: Demonstrates a classic deadlock scenario where two threads acquire two locks in opposite order, causing a circular wait condition.
examples.code:
import threading
import time

# Two shared resources (locks)
lock_a = threading.Lock()
lock_b = threading.Lock()

def thread_one():
    print("Thread 1: Trying to acquire lock A")
    lock_a.acquire()
    print("Thread 1: Acquired lock A")
    time.sleep(0.1)  # Simulate some work
    
    print("Thread 1: Trying to acquire lock B")
    lock_b.acquire()
    print("Thread 1: Acquired lock B")
    
    # Critical section
    lock_b.release()
    lock_a.release()

def thread_two():
    print("Thread 2: Trying to acquire lock B")
    lock_b.acquire()
    print("Thread 2: Acquired lock B")
    time.sleep(0.1)  # Simulate some work
    
    print("Thread 2: Trying to acquire lock A")
    lock_a.acquire()
    print("Thread 2: Acquired lock A")
    
    # Critical section
    lock_a.release()
    lock_b.release()

# Create and start threads
t1 = threading.Thread(target=thread_one)
t2 = threading.Thread(target=thread_two)

t1.start()
t2.start()

t1.join()
t2.join()
print("Program completed (will never reach here if deadlock occurs)")
examples.explanation: This example demonstrates the four necessary conditions for deadlock: 1) Mutual Exclusion (locks can only be held by one thread), 2) Hold and Wait (threads hold resources while waiting for others), 3) No Preemption (locks cannot be forcibly taken), 4) Circular Wait (Thread 1 waits for lock B held by Thread 2, while Thread 2 waits for lock A held by Thread 1). Thread 1 acquires lock A then waits for lock B, while Thread 2 acquires lock B then waits for lock A - creating a circular wait deadlock.

examples.title: Deadlock with Multiple Resources - Dining Philosophers Problem
examples.description: Implements the classic Dining Philosophers problem showing deadlock when all philosophers pick up their left fork simultaneously.
examples.code:
import threading
import time
import random

# Number of philosophers
N = 5
forks = [threading.Lock() for _ in range(N)]

def philosopher(philosopher_id):
    left_fork = philosopher_id
    right_fork = (philosopher_id + 1) % N
    
    while True:
        # Thinking
        print(f"Philosopher {philosopher_id} is thinking")
        time.sleep(random.uniform(0.1, 0.3))
        
        # Pick up forks (left first, then right)
        print(f"Philosopher {philosopher_id} tries to pick up left fork {left_fork}")
        forks[left_fork].acquire()
        print(f"Philosopher {philosopher_id} picked up left fork {left_fork}")
        
        print(f"Philosopher {philosopher_id} tries to pick up right fork {right_fork}")
        forks[right_fork].acquire()
        print(f"Philosopher {philosopher_id} picked up right fork {right_fork}")
        
        # Eating
        print(f"Philosopher {philosopher_id} is eating")
        time.sleep(random.uniform(0.1, 0.3))
        
        # Put down forks
        forks[right_fork].release()
        forks[left_fork].release()
        print(f"Philosopher {philosopher_id} put down forks")

# Create philosopher threads
# Note: This creates a deadlock when all threads start and acquire left forks
philosophers = [threading.Thread(target=philosopher, args=(i,)) for i in range(N)]

for p in philosophers:
    p.start()

# Run for a limited time to demonstrate deadlock
time.sleep(3)
print("Demo ended - in real scenario, this would deadlock")
examples.explanation: This demonstrates the Dining Philosophers deadlock scenario. Each philosopher picks up their left fork (forks[0] to forks[4]) before any can pick up their right fork. Since all philosophers hold their left fork and wait for their right fork (which is held by their neighbor), a circular wait deadlock occurs. This is a classic Operating Systems problem illustrating resource allocation and deadlock conditions. The four Coffman conditions for deadlock are all satisfied here.

examples.title: Deadlock Prevention - Using Timeout and Lock Ordering
examples.description: Demonstrates deadlock prevention techniques using timeout (try_lock) and consistent lock ordering to break the circular wait condition.
examples.code:
import threading
import time

# Solution 1: Using timeout (try_lock) to prevent deadlock
lock_x = threading.Lock()
lock_y = threading.Lock()

def safe_operation_with_timeout():
    # Try to acquire locks with timeout
    acquired_x = lock_x.acquire(timeout=1.0)
    if not acquired_x:
        print("Failed to acquire lock_x, retrying...")
        return
    
    try:
        print("Acquired lock_x")
        time.sleep(0.1)
        
        # Use try_lock with timeout instead of blocking acquire
        acquired_y = lock_y.acquire(timeout=1.0)
        if not acquired_y:
            print("Failed to acquire lock_y, releasing lock_x and retrying...")
            return  # Could implement retry logic here
        
        try:
            print("Acquired lock_y - Critical section completed safely")
        finally:
            lock_y.release()
    finally:
        lock_x.release()

# Solution 2: Using consistent lock ordering to prevent circular wait
account_a = {'lock': threading.Lock(), 'balance': 1000}
account_b = {'lock': threading.Lock(), 'balance': 1000}

def transfer_with_ordering(from_acc, to_acc, amount):
    # Always acquire locks in consistent order (by id)
    first_lock = from_acc['lock'] if id(from_acc) < id(to_acc) else to_acc['lock']
    second_lock = to_acc['lock'] if id(from_acc) < id(to_acc) else from_acc['lock']
    
    first_lock.acquire()
    try:
        second_lock.acquire()
        try:
            # Perform transfer
            if from_acc['balance'] >= amount:
                from_acc['balance'] -= amount
                to_acc['balance'] += amount
                print(f"Transferred {amount} from account {id(from_acc)} to {id(to_acc)}")
            else:
                print("Insufficient balance")
        finally:
            second_lock.release()
    finally:
        first_lock.release()

# Test deadlock prevention
print("=== Testing Timeout Solution ===")
t1 = threading.Thread(target=safe_operation_with_timeout)
t2 = threading.Thread(target=safe_operation_with_timeout)
t1.start()
t2.start()
t1.join()
t2.join()

print("\n=== Testing Lock Ordering Solution ===")
def transfer_thread_1():
    transfer_with_ordering(account_a, account_b, 100)

def transfer_thread_2():
    transfer_with_ordering(account_b, account_a, 50)

t3 = threading.Thread(target=transfer_thread_1)
t4 = threading.Thread(target=transfer_thread_2)
t3.start()
t4.start()
t3.join()
t4.join()

print(f"Final account A balance: {account_a['balance']}")
print(f"Final account B balance: {account_b['balance']}")
print("\nDeadlock prevented - program completed successfully!")
examples.explanation: This example demonstrates two practical deadlock prevention techniques: 1) Timeout-based prevention: Using try_lock with timeout allows threads to detect potential deadlocks and release held resources before waiting indefinitely. This breaks the 'Hold and Wait' condition. 2) Lock ordering: By enforcing a consistent global ordering on lock acquisition (based on object id), we break the 'Circular Wait' condition. Both threads acquire locks in the same order (first lock on lower id, second on higher id), preventing circular wait. These are standard techniques in operating systems to handle resource allocation problems.
